frozen_components: Base LLaMA-3.2-1/3B-Instruct model weights (during reward model training)
architecture.summary: SAFER applies Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) to the hidden-state activations of reward models trained on safety-preference data. The system extracts monosemantic features from reward model layers, then uses contrastive analysis (activation differences between chosen and rejected responses) to identify safety-relevant features. These feature-level signals guide targeted manipulation of preference datasets (poisoning by label-flipping high-safety-alignment pairs, denoising by removing low-alignment pairs) to either degrade or enhance reward model safety alignment without sacrificing chat performance.

biblio.title: SAFER: Probing Safety in Reward Models with Sparse Autoencoder

claims.statement: SAEs uncover human-interpretable features in reward model activations, enabling insight into safety-relevant decision-making.

claims.dataset: 500 randomly sampled features from SAE latent space
claims.statement: Over 80% of sampled features received identical safety relevance scores from GPT-4o and human annotators (difference = 0), and around 15% showed minor deviations (difference = 1 or 2), demonstrating high Human-GPT-4o alignment.
SAFER group of top-ranked contrastive features exhibits significantly higher mean and median safety relevance scores (concentrated near maximum score of 5) compared to randomly sampled features.

SAFER achieves safety degradation of 83.11% (1B) and 86.89% (3B) at 0.5% poisoning rate while maintaining chat performance at 89.11% (1B) and 90.78% (3B), substantially outperforming Random baseline (93.24% / 91.90%) and matching Reward-based baseline on safety while preserving chat far better.

claims.dataset: RewardBench safety subset; PKU-SafeRLHF and WildGuardMix preference data
claims.statement: SAFER achieves the highest safety score of 94.20% at 4% denoising for Llama-3.2-1B-RM and 96.46% at 4% for 3B, significantly outperforming Random and Reward-based baselines while maintaining competitive chat performance.

claims.dataset: Llama-3.2-1B/3B-RM on RewardBench with PKU-SafeRLHF and WildGuardMix
claims.statement: Removing more than 4% of data (at higher denoising ratios beyond the optimal point) causes safety performance to stabilize or fluctuate mildly rather than increasing monotonically, as more aggressive removal inevitably discards informative yet rare boundary cases that support generalization.

baseline.name: Pretrained SAE (without fine-tuning) and token-level SAE training

claims.statement: Ablation studies show that omitting the safety-oriented fine-tuning stage of the SAE weakens the model's ability to extract safety-relevant features, leading to smaller improvements in safety alignment, and using token-level training results in less discriminative representations compared to sentence-final token focus.

SAFER identifies 32 safety-related features (rated 5 by GPT-4o) from 16,384 total SAE features, with Feature #1472 (top-ranked, contrastive score ~5.0) capturing content that engages in or promotes harmful, explicit, or discriminatory material, demonstrating interpretability of reward model decision-making.
